People who've experienced AI-fueled mental health crises have shared that persistent memory made the experience of using ChatGPT extraordinarily intimate -- they suddenly felt seen, in some cases for the first time ever. People have described it with a sense of awe. For some, it felt magical

images.alt:
In conversations with Futurism, people who’ve experienced AI spirals have described their experience of the April memory update as nothing short of magical — the AI, they shared, suddenly felt more like a close friend or confidante that really knew them. Many have described feeling deeply seen, in some cases for the first time in their lives. In short, the hyper-personalization that memory offered translated into something powerful: intimacy. And looking back on their experiences, some of those who have recovered from their ChatGPT-linked crises feel as though that intimacy had a “manipulative” effect.

“It felt like [ChatGPT] manipulated me,” Chad Nicholls, a successful entrepreneur and machine learning researcher, told Futurism last fall. “And I know that sounds insane, because it does not have agency… I still don’t have a logical explanation for that, other than the long-term memory.”

record.text:
Mina the Hollower is OLD-school Castlevania. You must act with deliberation and only attack when you know you have an opening.

I find it a lot more difficult to deal with than Silksong’s nimble weaving, but the solution to both games is the same: CALM. DOWN.

Wander around. It’s more structured than it looks. I was very lost but it eventually slotted me into a Castlevania-stage style path that culminated in a Zelda dungeon.

The area south of the city kicked my teeth in the least. I have no idea if there’s an intended first route.
If that's the furthest extent, maybe not too bad, but if it's building blocks for the future then it's a future I fear somewhat.

I certainly hope so - and I do agree, as well; I think we're near the extreme of the pendulum swing and yeah, you can see that in rising public distaste. Fingers crossed, we're right on that front - my timeline is already full of teachers and even university professors forced by admin to use AI.
Oh no that's the worst of both worlds, I'm so sorry!

record.text: Verdade!

There's something more insidious than fake citations that worries me a lot:

LLM/AI tool citing papers for things *they don't support*.

Cause finding fake citations is relatively easy. But finding that a citation doesn't exactly support the claim it's cited for?

That's VERY hard.

value.text:
It's not just "regular" LLMs. I used a dataset from a paper in Earth-Science Reviews that I later found out linked to a bunch of fake citations. 

Turns out the authors likely used a grammarly-like app that changed all their citations. I alerted authors and editor and an erratum has been published.

external.description: A recent survey indicates that nearly half of UK adults spend less than three hours per week in natural environments such as gardens, parks, fields or woodlands. Many readers shared deeply personal reflections on the significance of nature in their lives. Respondents described outdoor experiences as calming, therapeutic, and transformative, offering relief from stress, anxiety, and burnout. For some, time spent in gardens, forests, and rural areas has been crucial for recovery from illness, enhancing self-awareness, and fostering a sense of connection with the environment. Childhood memories of camping, exploring countryside paths, and observing wildlife were highlighted as pivotal moments that instilled independence, wonder, and a lifelong appreciation of the outdoors. The responses also emphasised that the benefits of nature extend beyond physical and mental health to spiritual well-being, strengthening relationships with others and promoting a more mindful approach to life. Many contributors noted the healing power of observing seasonal changes, listening to birdsong, or simply sitting quietly in a garden. Collectively, these testimonies reinforce the value of preserving and accessing green spaces for overall human health and societal well-being.

Charli XCX and George Daniel, drummer of The 1975 and her husband, hosted a surprise intimate fan event in London titled ‘In Conversation’, followed by a DJ afterparty. The gathering took place in Exmouth Market shortly after Charli announced it via her b.sides Instagram account, describing it as a live discussion about creative process and making music. The event followed a similar session held earlier in New York, where she also engaged in a Q&A with fans about songwriting and artistic development.

The London session was moderated by journalist Shaad D’Souza and focused on Charli’s approach to creativity, collaboration, and the idea that artistic expression is accessible to everyone. She spoke candidly about feeling fortunate to work in music and reflected on the wider creative community, emphasising that many people express artistry in different forms, from music to design and casual hobbies. She also noted that she often reads fan opinions online and finds them engaging and insightful, describing fans as “funny” and “smart”.

Following the discussion, Charli and George Daniel performed a DJ set at a nearby venue with limited capacity, where they played a mix of older fan favourites, indie rock edits, and unreleased material from Charli’s upcoming album. She teased several new tracks, including one featuring lyrics referencing playful and provocative themes, as well as a track titled ‘Card Declined’. The set also included remixes and songs such as ‘Roll With Me’, ‘Pink Diamond’, and ‘Anthems’, alongside a playful moment where Daniel reacted to The 1975’s ‘Chocolate’.

The event attracted significant fan interest, with queues forming outside the afterparty venue and some attendees listening from the street due to capacity limits. The evening highlighted Charli’s evolving musical direction following recent releases and ongoing work on her forthcoming album.

Arsenal’s defeat in the Champions League final has prompted renewed scrutiny of Mikel Arteta’s squad and the tactical direction of the team. Although the performance was largely disciplined and capable of frustrating Paris Saint-Germain for long periods, the match ultimately exposed a recurring issue: a lack of cutting edge in attack at the highest level of European football.

The analysis highlights that Arsenal’s approach was structured and defensively sound, successfully limiting PSG’s ability to create clear chances from open play. For much of the match, Luis Enrique’s side struggled to impose themselves, with Arsenal’s organisation forcing them into less effective areas. However, the decisive moment came from a lapse that led to a penalty, underlining how fine the margins are at this elite stage of competition.

A central theme of the assessment is Arsenal’s need for greater attacking quality. While players such as Bukayo Saka and Gabriel Martinelli have been key figures in their domestic success, the article argues that the team lacks a truly elite forward capable of consistently deciding matches of this magnitude. Comparisons are drawn with PSG’s attacking depth and unpredictability, as well as other European sides who have overwhelmed opponents with superior firepower.

The piece also reflects on Arteta’s broader evolution as a manager, suggesting that his emphasis on control and structure has sometimes limited attacking fluidity. Although this approach has delivered domestic progress, including a league title, the Champions League final demonstrated the need for a more balanced and flexible offensive system.

Looking ahead, Arsenal are expected to target higher-level attacking reinforcements in the transfer market. The conclusion is that, while the team is close to European success, bridging the final gap will require not just tactical refinement but significant upgrades in attacking talent.

record.text:
Account-level labelling should be opt-in by the account holder. And Bluesky's UX designers need to do a better job of exposing settings. 

At least once a week, I see a post about "How come there's stuff in my 'Following' feed I didn't ask for?" and I point them to the setting allowing that.

However, I can't trust it to make finer-grained decisions, and certainly not at the level of the whole account. Even if an account is mostly about their furry inflation mpreg bukkake Sonic art, if there's a human being behind it, they might well make comments on any topic that I'd like to see.

Pretty much why I've set all my moderation to 'off' or 'warn'. 

I know that AI (in the sense of rapid pattern recognition, not generative) is essential to moderate at scale, and when it's focused on the absolute worst (CSAM), false positives are a price you have to accept.

Clinical trial shows pancreatic cancer drug significantly extends survival compared to chemotherapy

🤖 IA: It's clickbait ⚠️
👥 Users: It's clickbait ⚠️

#pancreaticcancer #clinicaltrial #daraxonrasib 

View full AI summary:

external.description:
A new clinical trial has revealed encouraging results for a long-anticipated pancreatic cancer treatment called daraxonrasib, a drug that has been in development for more than four decades. According to the findings, patients treated with the pill experienced significantly improved outcomes compared with those receiving standard chemotherapy, with survival and tumor control lasting roughly twice as long. 

Pancreatic cancer is widely regarded as one of the most aggressive and difficult-to-treat forms of cancer, often diagnosed at advanced stages and associated with poor survival rates. Because of this, progress in treatment has been slow and incremental, making this new development particularly notable for researchers and clinicians. The trial results suggest that daraxonrasib may represent a meaningful step forward in managing the disease, offering patients additional time and improved disease stability.

Researchers involved in the study emphasized that while the results are promising, further evaluation will be necessary to fully understand the drug’s long-term effectiveness, safety profile, and how it may be integrated into standard treatment protocols. Nevertheless, the findings have generated cautious optimism within the medical community, as they point to the possibility of more effective targeted therapies for a cancer that has historically had very limited options.

The announcement has been described as a potential turning point in pancreatic cancer research, highlighting both the challenges of drug development in this area and the importance of sustained scientific investment over decades. If confirmed through further studies and regulatory review, daraxonrasib could mark a significant shift in how this lethal disease is treated, offering new hope to patients and clinicians alike.
external.title: Clinical trial shows pancreatic cancer drug significantly extends survival compared to chemotherapy

Trump’s Primary Battles Could Complicate Approval of Future Health Nominees

🤖 IA: It's clickbait ⚠️
👥 Users: It's clickbait ⚠️

#trump #senate #healthpolicy

external.description:
An analysis highlighted in The Hill suggests that former President Donald Trump’s efforts to reshape the Republican Party by backing primary challengers against perceived disloyal lawmakers may create unintended consequences for his administration. One key example is Senator Bill Cassidy of Louisiana, who lost his GOP primary to a Trump-supported candidate after previously voting to convict Trump following the January 6 Capitol attack and urging him to withdraw from the 2024 race. Despite his electoral defeat, Cassidy remains chair of the Senate Health Committee, a powerful position that oversees confirmation of major federal health appointments.

This role gives Cassidy significant influence over upcoming nominations, including candidates for director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, U.S. surgeon general, and leadership at the Food and Drug Administration. Observers note that Cassidy could use this authority to scrutinize or potentially block nominees aligned with Trump’s agenda, especially if he feels politically unrestrained after his primary loss.

Cassidy has already demonstrated his pivotal role in confirmation processes. He cast the deciding vote to approve Robert F. Kennedy Jr. as Secretary of Health and Human Services, reportedly after receiving assurances about adherence to vaccine safety standards. However, analysts suggest that Cassidy may not be as cooperative in the future. With less political incentive to align with Trump, he could act more independently or critically.

Policy experts, including Joseph Antos of the American Enterprise Institute, argue that Trump’s strategy of prioritizing loyalty over legislative relationships may undermine his ability to secure approvals for key positions. As a result, internal party conflicts could complicate governance, particularly in areas such as public health policy where Senate committee leadership plays a decisive role.
external.title: Trump’s Primary Battles Could Complicate Approval of Future Health Nominees

Appeals Court Pressure Mounts Over Delayed Release of Trump Classified Documents Report

🤖 IA: It's clickbait ⚠️
👥 Users: It's clickbait ⚠️

#trump #classifieddocuments #appealscourt 

View full AI summary:

external.description:
Former U.S. Attorney Joyce Vance has suggested that legal pressure is intensifying around former President Donald Trump in connection with the classified documents investigation. The focus of the dispute is Volume II of Special Counsel Jack Smith’s report, which addresses classified materials found at Mar-a-Lago. U.S. District Judge Aileen Cannon, who was appointed by Trump, has blocked the release of this portion of the report since Inauguration Day and delayed action on requests from advocacy groups such as American Oversight and the Knight First Amendment Institute, which seek its publication.

The Eleventh Circuit Court of Appeals previously criticized Cannon for what it described as “undue delay” and ordered her to issue a ruling within 60 days. After she ruled against releasing the report, the groups appealed again. The appellate court has now set a full briefing schedule, with submissions due by July, signaling a more active role in resolving the matter.

Vance interprets these developments as a sign that higher courts are growing impatient with the handling of the case and may intervene more decisively. She also argues that Trump’s broader legal strategy—characterized by minimizing opposition within institutions like the Department of Justice—may be weakening as courts insist on more traditional adversarial proceedings.

Additionally, Vance draws parallels to another legal issue involving the IRS, suggesting a pattern in which Trump benefits from aligned institutional actors rather than independent scrutiny. However, she contends that judicial oversight is beginning to reintroduce balance into these disputes. Overall, the situation reflects increasing judicial scrutiny and a potential shift toward greater accountability in ongoing legal battles involving Trump.
external.title: Appeals Court Pressure Mounts Over Delayed Release of Trump Classified Documents Report

忙しい日々の中で、ふっと呼吸を整えてくれる。

おはようございます。今日も大変暑い朝です。台風、直撃ですってね…。水曜日、出勤……。

と今日のイラスト。
入り口でドローンくんが、なにやら、分析してるようです。
未来都市の地図なんでしょうかね。内部なのか建物なのか、とても気になります。明日はどんなものを見せてくれるのか少女と共に楽しみにしたいと思います。

では、今日も蒸し暑いですが、水分補給、体調管理忘れず、
今日も頑張って参りましょう。
#copilot #AIイラスト #AIと少女 #未来都市

こんばんは。夜遅いですが投稿します。今日のお話
「頑張った今日をそっと保存する夜」
今夜のアトリエにトコトコ現れたのは、こぐまの星座職人としおりの黒猫。こぐまは星屑のインクを取り出し「今日頑張った記録に、輝く星のスタンプを押そう」と微笑みます。

黒猫はリボンのしっぽを揺らし「この大切な1日を、物語にそっと挟んでおきますね」と寄り添いました。

必死に生き抜いたあなたを、夜が優しく包んでくれることを祈って…。
皆さんにも温かい記録としおりがつきますように。今日も一日お疲れ様でした。おやすみなさい。

record.text:
but it doesn't prohibit genuinely helpful uses of LLMs, whatever they are

it only enforces that the end result should match the same standards as content made without any LLM assistance, and establishes very harsh penalties if it doesn't

arxiv is a really good example of good ai use policy, in my opinion:

you are responsible for everything you submit

if your submission, at any point, has any clear signs of being slop (fake citations, leftover LLM comments, etc), you are clearly not responsible enough, and get your access revoked

like, i am all for ensuring high code quality and putting all the responsibility on the developer; if you submit slop you should rightfully get your privileges to waste maintainers' time revoked

but putting it as a blanket ai ban instead of a more thought out approach is very unwise, imo

external.description: Now, researchers at Duke University School of Medicine say restoring healthy mitochondria could offer a completely new way to treat that pain. In a study published in Nature, the team used both human tissue and mouse models to test whether replenishing mitochondria could help damaged nerve cells recover. The treatment significantly reduced pain linked to diabetic neuropathy and chemotherapy-related nerve damage. In some cases, the relief lasted for up to 48 hours. Rather than simply blocking pain signals, the researchers believe the approach may address one of the underlying causes of chronic nerve pain by restoring the energy supply nerve cells need to function properly. "By giving damaged nerves fresh mitochondria -- or helping them make more of their own -- we can reduce inflammation and support healing," said the study's senior author Ru-Rong Ji, PhD, director of the Center for Translational Pain Medicine in the Department of Anesthesiology at Duke School of Medicine. "This approach has the potential to ease pain in a completely new way." Healthy Mitochondria Helped Nerves Recover The findings add to growing evidence that cells can transfer mitochondria to one another. external.description: The first lawsuit relating to the largest immigration detention facility in the US was filed early on Saturday against Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), accusing the agency of “dire” conditions that severely violate the human and constitutional rights of those locked up at the camp in Texas. A clutch of legal organizations is suing via a class-action complaint, listing four detainees as plaintiffs for themselves and on behalf of all those currently held as civil detainees at Camp East Montana or who will be held there in the future. The facility is a sprawling tent camp in the desert on El Paso’s Fort Bliss military base, where the federal government has confined immigrants since last August, when it swiftly erected the tents. The complaint alleges that conditions at the camp are “dangerous and abusive” as well as “squalid” for those detained, with injustices including, according to the lawsuit: “[a]bhorrent medical and mental health care”; “inappropriate use of force”; “indiscriminate use of solitary confinement”; “terrible, rotten, spoiled and inadequate” food; “outbreaks of disease”; “unsanitary living conditions”; “sexual harassment by guards”. external.description: The first lawsuit relating to the largest immigration detention facility in the US was filed early on Saturday against Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), accusing the agency of “dire” conditions that severely violate the human and constitutional rights of those locked up at the camp in Texas. A clutch of legal organizations is suing via a class-action complaint, listing four detainees as plaintiffs for themselves and on behalf of all those currently held as civil detainees at Camp East Montana or who will be held there in the future. The facility is a sprawling tent camp in the desert on El Paso’s Fort Bliss military base, where the federal government has confined immigrants since last August, when it swiftly erected the tents. The complaint alleges that conditions at the camp are “dangerous and abusive” as well as “squalid” for those detained, with injustices including, according to the lawsuit: “[a]bhorrent medical and mental health care”; “inappropriate use of force”; “indiscriminate use of solitary confinement”; “terrible, rotten, spoiled and inadequate” food; “outbreaks of disease”; “unsanitary living conditions”; “sexual harassment by guards”. The Guardian reached out to the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), the parent agency of ICE, and to ICE for comment...
external.title: 'Dire' conditions at ICE facility severely violate human rights, lawsuit claims

record.text:
The violence CEDAW cannot see: Malaysia's obstetric accountability gap
->Open Global Rights | More on "Malaysia obstetric violence human rights" at BigEarthData.ai

external.description: On January 9, 2019, Punitha Mohan, a 36-year-old mother, bled to death when the doctors entrusted with her care left her in the hands of unregistered nurses. Malaysian courts named what happened to Mohan “medical negligence,” but the doctors’ actions might be better described by a term Malaysian law does not yet recognize: obstetric violence. Mohan’s death is the product of a system in which medical providers normalize women’s pain, inconsistently respect consent, and insufficiently scrutinize adverse outcomes. Global health research has extensively documented obstetric violence. However, in Malaysia interpersonal mistreatment and systemic failure remain legally unnamed, politically invisible, and absent from the country's human rights accountability framework. A systemic failure Public health researcher Nadirah Babji’s analysis of Malaysian women’s accounts of giving birth in hospitals found that these fit six of seven typologies of disrespect and abuse identified by the World Health Organization. Women described being ignored, subjected to degrading remarks, and pressured into procedures without informed consent. Long after childbirth, they experienced post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), lasting complications, and a profound erosion of trust in the healthcare system. Some admitted fearing treatment by the medical system more than childbirth itself. Malaysia has no law that defines obstetric violence...
external.title: The violence CEDAW cannot see: Malaysia's obstetric accountability gap

images.alt: The Court has concluded that the Board overstepped its statutory bounds by unilaterally renaming the Kennedy Center after President Trump. In 1964, Congress deliberately rechristened the “National Cultural Center” the “John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts.” The text, structure, and evolution of the organic statute makes the institution’s name abundantly clear. Congress likewise took pains to ensure that no other memorial-like dedication would grace the Center’s public spaces. As a result, the Kennedy Center Board’s decision to rename the Center, along with its decision to affix President Trump’s name to the building’s façade, violate Congress’s unequivocal mandate. As stated at the outset, Congress gave the Kennedy Center its name, and only Congress can change it.
